name: Ruy Lopez
description: The Spanish Opening. One of the most deeply analyzed openings, where White pressures Black's center by targeting the knight defending e5.

moves.commentary: You know the start — grab that center.
They match. Time for the Ruy Lopez setup.
Knight out, eyeing e5.
They defend. Now here's where it gets interesting...
The Ruy Lopez! You're pinning the knight that defends e5. You're not threatening to win the pawn yet — but the pressure is real.
The Morphy Defense — they ask your bishop: 'where are you going?' You need to decide.
Keep the pin going. Retreating to a4 maintains the tension — you're not giving anything up yet.
They counterattack your e4 pawn. Good move from them — now castle quickly.
Get your king safe and connect your rooks. The rook will support e4 from f1 or e1.
They develop and prepare to castle too. Solid play.
Rook to e1 — now e4 is rock solid. You've built a really stable position.
They push you back and gain queenside space. Your bishop needs to retreat.
The bishop lands on a great diagonal — still staring at f7. From here, the real strategic battle begins.
Center pawn. Here we go.
Symmetrical.
Knight out.
They defend e5.
Ruy Lopez bishop.
The Berlin Defense! They skip a6 and immediately counterattack your e4 pawn. This is called the 'Berlin Wall' — famously solid.
Castle and dare them to take on e4. If they do, you get active play.
They grab the pawn. Now you need to open things up fast before they consolidate.
Blast the center open! Your pieces will come to life.
Knight retreats, hitting your b5 bishop. They're fighting back.
Take the knight — yes, you're giving up the bishop pair, but you wreck their pawn structure.
Doubled pawns for them. They do get the bishop pair though — it's a tradeoff.
Win back your pawn. The position is opening up.
Their knight hops to an annoying square. Active piece.
Trade queens. Welcome to the Berlin endgame — don't let the quiet fool you. There's tons of play here.
They lose castling rights, but in this structure the king is actually fine in the center. A famous, complex endgame ahead.
Standard start.
They mirror.
Knight out.
Defense.
Ruy Lopez.
Morphy Defense.
Maintain the pressure.
They counterattack e4.
Castle. Safe and strong.
They prepare to castle too.
Reinforce e4 — your standard setup.
They push you back.
Bishop retreats to the strong diagonal.
Both sides castled. Now the real fight starts.
Prepare d4 — you want to expand your center.
WHOA — the Marshall Attack! They sacrifice a pawn for a vicious kingside attack. One of the most famous gambits in chess. Be ready.
Take it. Declining gives them an easy game. You need to accept and defend precisely.
They recapture with the knight, aiming to swing it to f4 or e3. A storm is coming at your king — but you're a pawn up. Stay sharp.
Center pawn.
They match.
Knight attacks e5.
They defend.
Ruy Lopez.
Morphy Defense.
Keep the tension.
Counterattack on e4.
Castle.
They develop.
Support e4.
They push your bishop.
Strong diagonal retreat.
The Closed Ruy Lopez — they lock the center down. This is a long strategic battle, not a quick tactical fight. Patience wins here.
Prepare d4. You're building up slowly — classic Ruy Lopez strategy.
They castle. Both sides are set up.
Key move! Stop their bishop from pinning your knight with Bg4. This little pawn move is essential in the Closed Ruy.
Wait, the knight goes BACK? Yep — the Breyer maneuver. It's heading to d7, then maybe f8-g6 or supporting d5. Deep stuff.
